title: PHI Legacy in Wang Laboratories: Charles Kenney's 'Riding the Runaway Horse' — Chapter 4 'I Have to Do This to Save My Company' (1995)
author: Charles C. Kenney
abstract: Excerpt from Charles C. Kenney's 1992-1995 book 'Riding the Runaway Horse: The Rise and Decline of Wang Laboratories' (Little, Brown). Documents PHI Computer Services as 'one of the best' early-1960s software pioneers; describes Wang Laboratories' 1968 PHI acquisition; identifies Bob Siegel, Harold Koplow, Dave Moros, and Bob Kolk — three of whom came from PHI — as the four men whose products yielded 'billions of dollars of revenues for Wang Laboratories' (Wang 2200 small-business computer, Wang Word Processing System, Wang VS commercial mini). Argues Wang's autobiography 'Lessons' significantly understates the PHI contribution to Wang's success.
importance_rationale: Authoritative secondary-source documentation of PHI's foundational role in Wang Laboratories' success — counters Doctor Wang's 'Lessons' book which downplayed the PHI human-capital contribution.
relevance_rationale: Wang Labs' rise-and-fall remains a paradigmatic Massachusetts technology cautionary tale; the PHI->Wang lineage produced the Wang VS, 2200, and Word Processing System that were core to the 1976-1983 Wang stock 100x ($6.75 to $800).
prescience_rationale: Secondary-source historical narrative; no forward predictions made.
